title: W4 B Limits and Continuity
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 4B    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/9
 Limits of Functions




                      2/9
                            Limit of a Function

Let f : A → R and let {xk }∞
                           k=1 be a sequence in A.


Since xk ∈ A for all k, we can think of {f (xk )}∞
                                                 k=1 as a sequence of real

numbers.
                       A                       R
                        x1                     f (x1 )
                        x2                     f (x2 )
                        x3                     f (x3 )
                        x4        f :A→R
                                               f (x4 )
                        x5.                    f (x5 )
                          ..                   ..
                                                .




                                                                             3/9
                              Limit of a Function

Let f : A → R and let {xk }∞
                           k=1 be a sequence in A.


Since xk ∈ A for all k, we can think of {f (xk )}∞
                                                 k=1 as a sequence of real

numbers.
                         A                     R
                          x1                   f (x1 )
                          x2                   f (x2 )
                          x3                   f (x3 )
                          x4          f :A→R
                                               f (x4 )
                          x5.                  f (x5 )
                            ..                 ..
                                                .




Suppose xk → x, where x ∈ A. We can ask two relevant questions:
  ◦ Does the sequence {f (xk )}∞
                               k=1 also converge?

  ◦ If it does, is its limit equal to f (x)?



                                                                             3/9
                        Limit of a Function




Proposition 1

 Let A ⊂ R. The function f : A → R is continuous at x ∈ A if, and
 only if, for any sequence {xk }∞
                                k=1 in A that converges to x, we have


                         lim f (xk ) = f (x).
                         k→∞




                                                                        4/9
                              Limit of a Function




 Proposition 1

    Let A ⊂ R. The function f : A → R is continuous at x ∈ A if, and
    only if, for any sequence {xk }∞
                                   k=1 in A that converges to x, we have


                                lim f (xk ) = f (x).
                               k→∞




Intuitively, continuity allows us to“pass the limit” across the function:
                         lim f (xk ) = f lim xk = f (x).
                                                   
                       k→∞                k→∞




                                                                            4/9
                                     Proof

=⇒ Suppose f is continuous at x and let {xk }∞
                                             k=1 be any sequence that goes to x.
We want to show that f (xk ) → f (x).




                                                                                   5/9
                                      Proof

=⇒ Suppose f is continuous at x and let {xk }∞
                                             k=1 be any sequence that goes to x.
We want to show that f (xk ) → f (x).
  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0. By continuity of f at x, there exists δ > 0 such that
                      |x′ − x| < δ =⇒ |f (x′ ) − f (x)| < ϵ.




                                                                                   5/9
                                      Proof

=⇒ Suppose f is continuous at x and let {xk }∞
                                             k=1 be any sequence that goes to x.
We want to show that f (xk ) → f (x).
  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0. By continuity of f at x, there exists δ > 0 such that
                      |x′ − x| < δ =⇒ |f (x′ ) − f (x)| < ϵ.

  ◦ Since xk → x, for the above δ > 0 there exists some K such that for all k ≥ K
    we have |xk − x| < δ.

  ◦ We conclude that for all k ≥ K, we have |f (xk ) − f (x)| < ϵ.




                                                                                    5/9
                                      Proof

=⇒ Suppose f is continuous at x and let {xk }∞
                                             k=1 be any sequence that goes to x.
We want to show that f (xk ) → f (x).
  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0. By continuity of f at x, there exists δ > 0 such that
                      |x′ − x| < δ =⇒ |f (x′ ) − f (x)| < ϵ.

  ◦ Since xk → x, for the above δ > 0 there exists some K such that for all k ≥ K
    we have |xk − x| < δ.

  ◦ We conclude that for all k ≥ K, we have |f (xk ) − f (x)| < ϵ.

 ⇐= Suppose that f (xk ) → f (x), but assume by contradiction that f is not
continuous at x.
  ◦ There exists ϵ > 0 such that for each δ k = 1/k > 0, we can find xk with
    |xk − x| < k1 but for which |f (xk ) − f (x)| ≥ ϵ.




                                                                                    5/9
                                      Proof

=⇒ Suppose f is continuous at x and let {xk }∞
                                             k=1 be any sequence that goes to x.
We want to show that f (xk ) → f (x).
  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0. By continuity of f at x, there exists δ > 0 such that
                      |x′ − x| < δ =⇒ |f (x′ ) − f (x)| < ϵ.

  ◦ Since xk → x, for the above δ > 0 there exists some K such that for all k ≥ K
    we have |xk − x| < δ.

  ◦ We conclude that for all k ≥ K, we have |f (xk ) − f (x)| < ϵ.

 ⇐= Suppose that f (xk ) → f (x), but assume by contradiction that f is not
continuous at x.
  ◦ There exists ϵ > 0 such that for each δ k = 1/k > 0, we can find xk with
    |xk − x| < k1 but for which |f (xk ) − f (x)| ≥ ϵ.

  ◦ We have found a sequence {xk } that converges to x yet it is not true that
    f (xk ) → f (x).




                                                                                    5/9
                           Continuity Revisited


We can establish the continuity of functions in a simple way.


 Proposition 2

   Let f and g be two real-valued functions defined on A ⊆ R. If f and
   g are continuous at x ∈ A, then
    (a) f + g is continuous at x.

    (b) α f is continuous at x, for any α ∈ R.

    (c) f × g is continuous at x.

    (d) If g(x) ̸= 0, then f /g is continuous at x.




                                                                         6/9
                       Some Examples Revisited


Example 1

  (a) g1 defined by                
                                    − x2        for x < 0,
                       g1 (x) =
                                   x + 1        for x ≥ 0
                                            10


      is not continuous at x = 0.

 (b) g2 (x) = a0 + a1 x + a2 x2 + a3 x3 is continuous.
               x3     √
 (c) g3 (x) = x+5  − x is continuous.

 (d) g4 (x) = |x| is continuous.




                                                              7/9
                The Euclidean Norm is Continuous

 Proposition 3

   Let f : Rn → R be defined by f (x) = ∥x∥. Then f is continuous.


Proof Recall that f is defined by
                                    Xn           1
                                                   2
                        f (x) =             x2j        .
                                      j=1


  ◦ Take a sequence {xk }∞                  n
                         k=1 of vectors in R converging to x.




                                                                     8/9
                  The Euclidean Norm is Continuous

 Proposition 3

   Let f : Rn → R be defined by f (x) = ∥x∥. Then f is continuous.


Proof Recall that f is defined by
                                          Xn           1
                                                         2
                                f (x) =           x2j        .
                                            j=1


  ◦ Take a sequence {xk }∞                  n
                         k=1 of vectors in R converging to x.

  ◦ We have xkj → xj for all j = 1, . . . , n, and therefore (xkj )2 → x2j .

  ◦ The sequence of real numbers (xk1 )2 + (xk2 )2 + . . . + (xkn )2 converges to
    x21 + x22 + . . . + x2n .

  ◦ The square root preserves the convergence.




                                                                                    8/9
                        Continuity Revisited



Proposition 4

 Let f and g be two real-valued functions defined on A ⊆ Rn . If f
 and g are continuous at x ∈ A, then
  (a) f + g is continuous at x.

  (b) α f is continuous at x, for all α ∈ R.

  (c) f × g is continuous at x.

  (d) If g(x) ̸= 0, then f /g is continuous at x.
